query: How does Vue handle conditional rendering and list rendering efficiently?

competitors.label: List Rendering
competitors.selectionReason: The official list rendering guide is the best single Vue page for keyed identity, v-for behavior, and the v-for with v-if priority pitfall behind this prompt.
theirStrengths: The docs are authoritative on v-for semantics, keys, and directive priority rules.
theirGaps: They are less compressed around the interview-ready decision rule across v-if, v-show, and v-for in one answer.

realWorldUseCases: v-if creates and destroys branches, v-show keeps DOM mounted and toggles visibility, and v-for depends on stable keys so Vue can patch identity correctly during list updates.
v-if adds or removes elements from the DOM completely based on the condition.
followUpConfusion: The :key attribute helps Vue track each item’s identity when updating the DOM.
This makes updates more efficient, as Vue can determine what has changed, added, or removed using its virtual DOM diffing algorithm.

realWorldUseCases: The official docs cover the directives accurately, but our page is stronger on the production framing that they solve different problems and should not be treated as interchangeable.
actionableExamples: The docs remain competitive on directive examples, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is clearer on the combined follow-up around mount/unmount, CSS-only toggling, and keyed identity in one place.

actionableExamples: If needed later, add one explicit wrapper-level v-if versus item-level v-if example to make the priority pitfall even faster to spot.

competitors.winReason: The official docs are stronger as a reference, but our page is stronger as a compact interview answer because it unifies v-if, v-show, and keyed v-for into one concrete decision rule.

competitors.label: Vue Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's Vue interview bank is directly relevant because rendering directives, list identity, and DOM-update behavior are common Vue interview themes.
theirStrengths: It is a strong broad interview-prep hub for Vue fundamentals.
theirGaps: The category page is broader than this exact v-if, v-show, and keyed list-rendering trade-off.

realWorldUseCases: the production pitfall is treating them as interchangeable.
Think of v-if as removing or inserting items from a shelf and v-show as simply covering or uncovering them — both are efficient, but suited for different situations.
followUpConfusion: Each list item is tracked using a key , allowing Vue to reuse and reorder DOM elements instead of re-creating them from scratch.
The :key attribute helps Vue track each item’s identity when updating the DOM.

realWorldUseCases: The hub is clearly relevant for Vue rendering questions, but it is less explicit about this exact efficiency trade-off.
actionableExamples: GreatFrontEnd remains competitive on example-driven interview practice, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: The category page is less specific about v-for plus v-if priority and why stable keys matter for identity rather than only performance.

actionableExamples: Keep the answer focused on one conditional/list decision rule instead of expanding into a general directives survey.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is useful as a Vue fundamentals bank, but our page is stronger because it answers the exact efficiency question directly and ties it to the highest-risk list and conditional pitfalls.

realWorldUseCases: It’s useful when the condition changes infrequently.
which is more efficient for frequent toggling.
followUpConfusion: Vue uses a virtual DOM to compare the current and previous states of the UI.
v-for efficiently renders dynamic lists using keys.
